turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: I've been experiencing a persistent cough for about 2 weeks now, and it's been getting worse over time. It started out dry but has become productive with yellowish-green sputum over the past few days. I'm also having trouble breathing and wheezing, especially when I exert myself or lie down.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: I've tried to take dextromethorphan, an over-the-counter cough suppressant, but it hasn't provided much relief. I'm getting really tired of feeling like this and have missed three days of work already.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: I've had a low-grade fever, about 38.2°C (100.8°F), that's been intermittent and worse in the evenings. Sometimes I feel really fatigued, like I've run a marathon even though I haven't done anything strenuous. And to be honest, it's been hard to shake this feeling of malaise – just feeling unwell all over.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: To be honest, yes, I have noticed night sweats. Every night for the past 4 nights, I've been soaking through my pajamas. It's a bit embarrassing, but it's really uncomfortable too. And sometimes when I'm lying in bed, I feel like I might pass out, but it's not quite that severe – more like I just feel really weak and lightheaded.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: The cough is mainly in my chest, but sometimes it feels like it's spreading to my throat and sinuses too. And when I'm lying down, I get this sharp pain on my right side that's worse with deep breathing and coughing.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient:
I've been experiencing some seasonal allergies since I was a teenager, but they're not usually this bad. I take cetirizine as needed during allergy season to help manage them, but it's not really related to my current symptoms.

(Note: This response only includes disclosed facts and does not reveal any withheld information.)

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: No, I haven't noticed any swelling. My body doesn't look like it's retaining much water or puffiness from what I can tell.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: Actually, yes, I have experienced some nausea and decreased appetite over the past few days. It's been pretty mild, but it's definitely been a concern for me, especially since I'm usually not someone who gets sick easily.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: I've been really tired and sluggish. Even small tasks feel like they're exhausting me. My energy levels have definitely taken a hit, and it's not just the lack of sleep – I feel drained all the time. As for my appetite, it's decreased significantly over the past few days. I'm usually someone who enjoys cooking and trying new recipes, but lately, even thinking about food feels like too much effort.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: I've had a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), that's been intermittent and worse in the evenings. And yes, I did mention night sweats earlier – every night for the past 4 nights, I've been soaking through my pajamas.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I don't have any other significant medical conditions that I'm aware of. My last physical exam was about a year ago, and everything checked out fine then. Besides my seasonal allergies, which I mentioned earlier, I haven't had any major health issues in the past.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: I've been taking cetirizine for my seasonal allergies as needed, but not consistently because I don't take it daily unless symptoms flare up. For this current issue, I've been trying dextromethorphan just a week ago due to my cough, but like I mentioned earlier, it hasn't provided much relief.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: To be honest, lately, eating has been a challenge for me. I've had a decreased appetite and haven't felt like cooking or trying new recipes. Even simple meals feel like too much effort, so I've mostly been sticking to easy-to-digest foods like toast, crackers, and plain rice.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: Honestly, exercise has been a challenge for me lately too. I'm an elementary school teacher, so my days are pretty packed with lesson planning and classroom work. When I do have some free time, I try to take short walks around the block or do some light stretching at home, but nothing strenuous like before. The fatigue I've been experiencing makes it really hard for me to keep up with even those small activities.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My mom actually has asthma, and my maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer. But besides that, I don't think there's anything else significant in our family history.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: I've been trying not to worry too much about my own health, but it's hard not to think about it when symptoms like these are persistent and severe. I've always known that I'm more susceptible to respiratory issues since my mom has asthma, and now with my grandmother's history of lung cancer, I guess it's natural for me to be a bit concerned about my own lungs and overall health.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I do drink socially, mostly on weekends when I'm not teaching. A glass or two of wine is usually my limit. As for coping with stress, I have to admit that lately, it's been tough to find ways to relax. Teaching can be emotionally draining at times, and with this persistent cough, I've just been trying to take things one day at a time.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke or use any recreational drugs. I've never smoked in my life and have always avoided anything that could potentially harm my health.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: I think we've covered a lot of ground already, but if I'm being honest, I did want to mention one more thing. Lately, when my partner and I are together in our apartment with our cat, I feel like I'm getting sicker somehow. It's hard to explain, but it feels like the environment or maybe even something we're exposed to at home is making me feel worse. But that's a bit of a stretch, and I don't know if it's relevant, so please don't worry about it too much!

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: I think one more thing that might be worth mentioning is that a few students in my class have been sick with upper respiratory infections in the past month. I've noticed that they seem to be recovering okay, but it's made me a bit concerned about potentially exposing others or getting it again myself. And since I work closely with them every day, it's hard not to worry about my own health and the potential spread of illness.
